caveats: a: Federal Postal Service staffing may differ substantially from contract postal units and outsourced route operators.
a: Occupational employment shares are not direct task-level AI exposure measures.
a: Sorting machinery and generative AI are distinct technologies, so mechanization evidence is used only to avoid crediting already-automated work twice.
rho: National Postal Service capital programs are not direct observations of private-contractor adoption.
rho: Implementation varies between retail contract units, sorting subcontractors, and route operators.
rho: Cybersecurity, labor rules, and Postal Service approval could slow deployment beyond a normal small-business software cycle.
e: The historical contract postal unit count is stale and includes outlets that may be ancillary operations rather than stand-alone acquisition candidates.
e: No observed evidence supports converting the known existence of contractors into an eligibility percentage.
e: The declared firm-population dataset gap is preserved rather than replaced with an inferred count.
s5: Contract duration does not establish that an operating company or its Postal Service contract can be sold.
s5: Historical outlet closures or count declines are not equivalent to control transactions.
s5: Any numeric sale-rate range would be unsupported while the eligible population and transfer rules remain unobserved.
q: The GAO economics are historical and do not measure current AI-related retention.
q: Capture may differ sharply between fixed-compensation units and volume- or performance-based arrangements.
q: A single dominant contracting system creates greater pass-through risk than a fragmented commercial customer base.
d5: The source forecast covers the national Postal Service, not demand specifically outsourced to private contractors.
d5: A constant compound conversion is a simplifying assumption; volume decline may be front- or back-loaded.
d5: Postal price increases can support nominal revenue even while constant-quality physical demand falls.
d5: Package activity faces competitive and network-strategy dynamics not captured by the mail forecast.
o: This estimate avoids counting electronic mail diversion again because that effect is already reflected in the demand factor.
o: Operator necessity may be lower for retail counter functions than for last-mile delivery.
o: A network redesign could shift work back to the Postal Service without eliminating the underlying operator function.

lens.customer_and_revenue_model: The Postal Service is the dominant contracting customer and postal patrons are the end users. Contract postal units charge Postal Service prices and receive contract compensation that may be performance-based or fixed; other included contractors earn recurring fees under Postal Service procurement arrangements.
lens.deviation_from_default: The lens is narrowed to private contract operators because the overwhelmingly visible industry operator is a non-acquirable federal establishment. This is necessary to define a coherent control-investment universe, not to select for attractiveness.
lens.excluded_activities: The U.S. Postal Service itself is excluded because it is a federal establishment rather than an acquirable private company. Also excluded are bulk mail transportation, private courier and express delivery, presort/consolidation/barcoding services, mailbox stores, ancillary postal counters that are not transferable standalone operations, shells, and non-control investments.
lens.included_activities: U.S. private lower-middle-market contractors actually classified in NAICS 491110 that operate contract postal units or perform recurring outsourced mail acceptance, sorting, routing, or delivery within the Postal Service's universal-service system.

a.bridge: BLS occupation employment and wage data describe the federal Postal Service, not the private contractor lens. I map the federal task mix to contractor work: carriers and much processing are physical, while clerk inquiry, address and exception handling, dispatch, routing, and administration are more exposed. Contractors may have less overhead to automate than the federal operator, biasing the proxy high, but smaller operators may also use more manual office processes, biasing it low.
caveats: Federal Postal Service staffing may differ substantially from contract postal units and outsourced route operators.
Occupational employment shares are not direct task-level AI exposure measures.
Sorting machinery and generative AI are distinct technologies, so mechanization evidence is used only to avoid crediting already-automated work twice.
a.change_evidence: Payroll by occupation and task for private 491110 contractors, paired with time-and-motion data separating physical handling from inquiry, dispatch, exception, and administrative work, would materially change this range.
a.rationale: The occupation mix is dominated by mail carriers and postal sorting/processing roles, constraining direct generative-AI exposure. A smaller share of labor cost sits in customer interaction, exception resolution, scheduling, routing support, and administration where AI can materially assist. Existing parcel-sorting investment also means some automation opportunity is already embodied in machinery rather than incremental AI labor substitution.

d5.bridge: The Postal Service OIG projects combined First-Class Mail and Marketing Mail volume over ten years, with a 29% baseline decline and a 14% to 41% range. I convert those endpoints to approximate five-year factors using a constant compound path, then temper the result using FY2025 mix: those two categories represented about 91% of total reported pieces, while packages and other products have different trajectories. This can understate demand for parcel-heavy contractors or overstate demand for contractors concentrated in declining letter mail.
caveats: The source forecast covers the national Postal Service, not demand specifically outsourced to private contractors.
A constant compound conversion is a simplifying assumption; volume decline may be front- or back-loaded.
Postal price increases can support nominal revenue even while constant-quality physical demand falls.
Package activity faces competitive and network-strategy dynamics not captured by the mail forecast.
d5.change_evidence: Contractor revenue and workload by mail class, route, retail transaction, and package activity, combined with updated Postal Service volume forecasts and outsourcing shares, would materially change the factor.
d5.rationale: Electronic diversion creates a structurally declining mail-piece base. The five-year range therefore centers below one, while allowing a less severe outcome for operators with package exposure and a more severe outcome for letter- and marketing-mail-heavy work. The measure is intended as constant-quality demand for the current service basket, not revenue growth from price increases.

caveats: The historical contract postal unit count is stale and includes outlets that may be ancillary operations rather than stand-alone acquisition candidates.
No observed evidence supports converting the known existence of contractors into an eligibility percentage.
The declared firm-population dataset gap is preserved rather than replaced with an inferred count.
e.change_evidence: A current Postal Service supplier and contract-unit roster joined to establishment identifiers, financials, ownership, NAICS confirmation, operating scope, and change-of-control eligibility could establish the eligible share.
e.rationale: A defensible share of firms meeting the acquisition lens cannot be established. Public evidence confirms that private contract postal operators exist, but it does not provide a current, deduplicated population linked to revenue, ownership, NAICS classification, stand-alone status, or transferability. The federal Postal Service must not be treated as an eligible firm.

l.detail: BLS Industry Productivity (IPS) direct: labor compensation (L02) $62,773.0M / sectoral output (T30) $44,545.8M, year 2024

n.detail: margin 0.0983 (margins.json[49]: Damodaran Jan 2026: Transportation EBITDA/Sales 9.83% (couriers/warehousing folded into transport aggregate)); revenue/employee $221,674 (SUSB 2022 receipts/employment at 48-49); DATA GAP: no SUSB size-class data for this code; n_band could not be computed — the research run must supply it with a shown chain, quality ESTIMATE
n.rationale: dataset n_band has no defensible value

caveats: This estimate avoids counting electronic mail diversion again because that effect is already reflected in the demand factor.
Operator necessity may be lower for retail counter functions than for last-mile delivery.
A network redesign could shift work back to the Postal Service without eliminating the underlying operator function.
o.change_evidence: Postal Service network plans identifying contractor displacement, self-service substitution rates, autonomous-handling deployments, contract-unit closures, and the share of remaining volume requiring human or entity-level operational accountability would materially change the estimate.
o.rationale: After demand contraction is handled separately, most remaining physical mail and package demand still requires an accountable operator for acceptance, processing, routing, or delivery under the universal-service system. Self-service, robotics, and network redesign could eliminate some contracted counter or processing roles, but they are unlikely to remove the operator requirement from most surviving physical flows within five years.

caveats: The GAO economics are historical and do not measure current AI-related retention.
Capture may differ sharply between fixed-compensation units and volume- or performance-based arrangements.
A single dominant contracting system creates greater pass-through risk than a fragmented commercial customer base.
q.change_evidence: Representative contracts showing fee formulas, reopeners, productivity clauses, audit rights, renewal pricing, gain-sharing, termination, and insourcing provisions, plus realized contractor margin bridges after automation, would materially change the range.
q.rationale: Contractors may retain savings inside fixed or performance-based compensation periods, but value capture is limited by a highly concentrated public-sector customer, Postal Service-set retail prices, competitive contracting, renewals and audits, procurement bargaining, and the possibility of insourcing or compensation reset. The wide range reflects contract-form variation and the absence of direct gain-sharing evidence.

rho.bridge: The Postal Service's documented deployment of sorting equipment, mobile devices, and information technology shows feasibility in the national network, while contract postal unit rules show that private operators work inside Postal Service-controlled prices and interfaces. I translate that deployment context into the fraction of exposed contractor opportunity implementable within five years. Large-system proof may overstate small-contractor integration capacity, while smaller organizations may adopt lightweight software faster than the federal operator.
caveats: National Postal Service capital programs are not direct observations of private-contractor adoption.
Implementation varies between retail contract units, sorting subcontractors, and route operators.
Cybersecurity, labor rules, and Postal Service approval could slow deployment beyond a normal small-business software cycle.
rho.change_evidence: Contractor-level software stacks, approved integration paths, AI pilot results, implementation budgets, data-access permissions, and measured adoption timelines would materially change the estimate.
rho.rationale: Implementability is moderate rather than high. Narrow office and exception workflows can adopt off-the-shelf tools, but contractor scope, Postal Service systems, procurement requirements, security, legacy interfaces, limited data control, and the physical nature of core work constrain implementation.

caveats: Contract duration does not establish that an operating company or its Postal Service contract can be sold.
Historical outlet closures or count declines are not equivalent to control transactions.
Any numeric sale-rate range would be unsupported while the eligible population and transfer rules remain unobserved.
s5.change_evidence: A longitudinal contractor registry matched to ownership transitions, brokered transactions, retirements, procurement novations, contract assignments, and Postal Service change-of-control approvals would materially change this assessment.
s5.rationale: There is no defensible evidence for the five-year share of eligible private postal contractors that would become actionable control opportunities. The eligible population is not enumerated, and available sources do not measure ownership succession, sale incidence, contract assignability, or change-of-control outcomes.

principal_driver: The principal positive driver is that surviving physical mail and package flows continue to require operational acceptance, processing, routing, and delivery, leaving a narrow but durable role for accountable operators and modest AI-enabled workflow savings.
principal_weakness: The principal weakness is firm availability: the federal operator is non-acquirable, while no defensible current evidence establishes how many private, appropriately sized, stand-alone, transferable postal contractors exist or are likely to sell.

sources.supports: Defines the industry as the Postal Service and subcontractors operating under a universal-service obligation; lists acceptance, collection, processing, routing, sorting, and delivery; and distinguishes excluded bulk transportation, presort/barcoding, and non-universal-service courier activities.
sources.title: 2022 NAICS Manual — Subsector 491 Postal Service and Industry 491110 Postal Service

sources.supports: Reports 632,810 jobs and a task mix dominated by 331,600 mail carriers, 116,520 mail sorters/processors/machine operators, and 78,130 postal clerks, with management a small share; used as a federal-operator proxy for labor-task exposure.
sources.title: May 2023 National Industry-Specific Occupational Employment and Wage Estimates: Postal Service (Federal Government)

sources.supports: Documents investment in information technology, mobile devices, and parcel sorting, including 348 new package sorting machines and higher daily capacity, while describing the continuing six-day mail and seven-day package service mandate.
sources.title: Delivering for America — Plan Details

sources.supports: Shows that supplier-operated contract postal units exist; they use supplier employees, charge Postal Service prices without surcharges, are competitively contracted, and may receive performance-based compensation.
sources.title: Becoming a Contract Postal Unit Supplier

sources.supports: Provides historical evidence that contract postal units were independent businesses compensated by the Postal Service, while showing varied fixed-compensation economics and a decline from 5,290 units in FY2002 to 3,619 in FY2011.
sources.title: U.S. Postal Service: Retail Facilities — Information on Cost and Accessibility

sources.supports: Reports FY2025 volumes by product, including 42.049 billion First-Class Mail, 56.756 billion Marketing Mail, 6.837 billion shipping and package pieces, and 108.695 billion total pieces; also identifies the Postal Service as an independent federal establishment.
sources.title: U.S. Postal Service Reports Fiscal Year 2025 Results

sources.supports: Projects combined First-Class and Marketing Mail volume from 2025 to 2035, with a 29% baseline decline and a 14% to 41% range, and identifies electronic diversion as the primary cause.
sources.title: Projecting Mail Volume: Future Trends and Implications for the Postal Service

title: Postal Service
